title: Present Simple Quiz
explanation: The third person singular (she/he/it) adds -es to verbs ending in -o, -ch, -sh, -ss, -x, or -z. 'Go' becomes 'goes'.
example1: She goes to school by bus.
example2: He goes to work at 8 AM.

title: Complete the Sentence
explanation: The base form 'play' is used with plural subjects (they, we, you). No -s or -es needed.

title: WH- Question Practice
question_text: Choose the correct WH- word for: _____ is your favorite color?
explanation: 'What' is used when asking about an unlimited range of options (any color). 'Which' would be used if there was a limited choice (e.g., red or blue).
tip: What = unlimited options; Which = limited options

title: Daily Routine Verbs
explanation: 'Wake up' is the base form. Use the base form after 'usually' with I/you/we/they. For he/she/it, use 'wakes up'.
example1: I wake up early every day.

title: Modal Perfect Practice
explanation: 'Should have + past participle' expresses regret about a past action. It means you didn't study, but you think it was the right thing to do.
example1: I should have called her yesterday.
example2: You should have told me earlier.
tip: Should have + past participle = past regret

title: Simple Present Practice
question_text: Choose the correct form: She _____ to school every day.
explanation: The third person singular (she/he/it) requires 'goes' in present simple. 'Every day' indicates a habit or routine.
example1: She goes to the gym every Monday.
example2: He goes to work by train.

title: Present Continuous Practice
question_text: Choose the correct form: I _____ (read) a book right now.
explanation: 'Right now' indicates an action happening at this moment. Use present continuous: am/is/are + verb-ing. 'I am reading' is correct.
example1: I am watching TV right now.
example2: She is cooking dinner at the moment.
usage1: Use present continuous for actions happening now.

title: Modal Probability - 80% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 80% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Should' expresses expectation or probability (around 80%). 'Will' is 100%. 'Must' is strong deduction (95%). 'Could' is 50% or less.
example1: The sky is dark. It should rain soon.
example2: He studied hard. He should pass the exam.

title: Modal Probability - 95% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 95% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Must' expresses strong logical deduction (about 95% certainty) when you have evidence. 'Will' is 100% (prediction). 'Should' is 80%. 'Could' is 50%.
example1: He hasn't eaten all day. He must be hungry.
example2: The ground is wet. It must have rained.

title: Modal Probability - 100% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 100% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Will' expresses a prediction with 100% certainty about the future. It's not a deduction like 'must', but a firm prediction.
example1: The sun will rise at 6:15 AM tomorrow.
example2: I will be 30 years old next month.

title: Modal Probability - 50% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 50% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Could' expresses possibility (around 50%). It means it's possible but not certain. The other modals express higher certainty.
example1: It could snow tonight, but it's not guaranteed.
example2: She could come to the party, or maybe not.

title: Modal Probability - 30% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 30% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Might' expresses weaker possibility (around 30-40%). (Note: 'could' was used above, but for 30% certainty, 'might' is most appropriate.)
example1: I might go to the concert, but I'm not sure.
example2: It might rain later, but probably not.

title: Modal Probability - 60% Certainty
question_text: Choose the correct modal for 60% certainty: It _____ rain tomorrow.
explanation: 'Should' expresses expected probability (60-80%). 'May' is also possible (50-60%). 'Will' is 100%. 'Must' is 95%. For 60%, 'should' or 'may' work.
example1: The forecast says it should rain by noon.
example2: The train should arrive in 10 minutes.

title: Stative vs Dynamic
question_text: Choose the correct form: I _____ (think / am thinking) that you are right.
explanation: 'Think' meaning 'believe' or 'have an opinion' is a stative verb. Stative verbs are not usually used in continuous form. Use present simple.
tip: Use present simple with stative verbs
